## [06:02 - 09:04]

How does that feel? Feels good. Yeah, I appreciate it. So what do you think? You want to do this? There it is. Let's go. All right. So let's start with you. What was your experience of that conversation? I think you're always going to have objections go from money and then sometimes a spouse or anything else. But a lot of that, really what I brought into there, is how to talk really to your spouse about it. Is it actually the support you need or is it permission you need? Because a lot of times it's just coming to them and saying, hey, it's $5,000. That's not a good pitch, right? You need to know how to really talk to them. And it's funny. We've been dating our spouse for a long time, but we still maybe don't know how to talk to him. But I think doing that is a huge part of this. And you're actually, he's on my side, saying, hey, let me help you out versus kind of being the bad sales person that says, hey, I don't care what your wife thinks. Hey, let me help you of how to talk to your wife. So I think it was super good. It wasn't pressure at all. And I actually did feel like the man when I was done with that. Yeah. I think the main thing here is there's a tendency for people that are just starting out in sales. If there's any objection, hey, the money or it's the wife, there's this tendency to be like, oh, OK, yeah, yeah, I understand. Oh, yeah, no problem. I don't want to intrude. But that is not serving my client, my prospect, my guy, that I want to more than anything help this man. And I can see where he's falling short. And so my role is to ask certain questions, to get him to a place to, again, boost his resolve so he can feel confident and he can feel more certain within himself. And if he were to keep going back and forth, if there was some logistics, like, I just don't have $5,000 in the bank account. I don't know how. Like, I'm down to pay it. I just don't know how. Well, then I might break it up, or I might figure out some sort of creative way to lower that barrier of entry so he can feel good and feel confident about saying yes. And you can always tell two for a money objection. If it's completely out of the picture, and I kind of gave him some big eyes at the beginning, but usually they'll go, yeah, no. That one is a logistics. They cannot afford that whatsoever. But if they're ever hemming and hawing, like, hmm, I don't know. That's where you just kind of dig in a little bit more. Ask them some more questions. Get them to think differently about this, because that doesn't mean it's a no. No. The most support you can possibly give somebody is to allow them just to be in that silence and be with their thoughts and their emotions and their whatever. You have no idea what's happening.
